CARL WILLIAM MUSIC
DIY Live Rig — Open Source Effects Platform
RASPBERRY PI 4
PEDALBOARD
Not for the faint of heart.
> The rig. Battery powered. Ready to go.

This started as a question: what if an $80 computer could replace a pedalboard worth ten times that — and sound better doing it?

The Raspberry Pi 4 Pedalboard is a fully portable, battery powered effects rig built around MODEP — an open source modular effects platform that runs hundreds of LV2 plugins on hardware that fits in the palm of your hand. Plug in a guitar. Step on a switch. The sound coming out the other end doesn't have to sound like anything that's ever existed before.

That's the point. Commercial units are designed around sounds most players want most of the time. The weird edges get sanded off. This rig has no such constraints. Granular pitch freezers, spectral smearers, convolution reverbs loaded with impulse responses of actual physical spaces, experimental plugins that no commercial company would ever ship as a product. If someone built it and released it as an LV2 plugin, it runs here.

NO AMP. NO PEDALBOARD. NO WALL POWER.
JUST A LITTLE BOX AND WHATEVER SOUND YOU CAN IMAGINE.

Getting here wasn't painless. There was a lot of troubleshooting. USB bus architecture, power supply quality, real time kernel scheduling, battery bank regulation. The kind of quality you expect from a commercial product but DIY gear makes you earn. In the end it does something no off-the-shelf pedal can, an entire virtual pedalboard that you can customize.

A USB foot switch and numpad give hands-free control over effect switching, parameter changes, and preset loading. All inputs are bridged to MIDI with a custom Python script I wrote. The whole thing runs off a single Anker battery bank. Fits in a bag, ready to grab and go anywhere.

  • Raspberry Pi 4
  • Desk Pi Pro enclosure
  • Focusrite Scarlett 2i4 2nd gen
  • MODEP on Patchbox OS
  • USB foot switch (Linemaster)
  • USB numpad controller
  • Custom Python HID -> MIDI bridge
  • Single Anker battery bank

People see it on stage and don't know what they're looking at. Then they hear it.
